name: Cotton T-Shirt
description:
Resource cycle for a conventional cotton T-shirt.

Graph nodes show rolled-up CO₂e (emissions only). Click a node for the step breakdown. The statement table compares Manufacture / Recycle / Reuse / Landfill pathways. Transformation is where ready fabric becomes the finished garment for the wearer.
co2e_basis: Absolute process emissions only (no avoided-burden credits). Illustrative order-of-magnitude from apparel LCAs — not a certified EPD. Virgin path ≈ fibre farming + mill/dye/sew; recycle ≈ collect + fibre recovery; reuse ≈ sort + second-hand logistics. All routes emit CO₂e; reuse < recycle < virgin manufacture.

nodes.label: Raw Material
nodes.summary: Grow, pick, gin, and bale cotton fibre
nodes.details:
Cotton monocultures often need irrigation, fertilizer, and pesticides.

1. Grow and mechanically harvest seed cotton.
2. Gin to separate lint from seed; bale lint for spinning mills.
3. Truck fibre to yarn mills.

Fertilizer N₂O and irrigation energy usually dominate field GHG.
nodes.co2e_note: ~2.4 kg CO₂e/shirt for field + ginning + fibre haul (illustrative; irrigated conventional cotton).

nodes.label: Manufacture
nodes.summary: Spin, knit/weave, dye, cut, and sew
nodes.details:
1. Spin lint into yarn; knit or weave fabric.
2. Wet processing / dyeing (often the largest mill GHG).
3. Cut and sew the garment; finish and pack empty (unlabelled) blanks or finished blanks for transformation.

Recycled fibre rejoins spinning (folded into Recycle → Transformation as recovered fabric ready to finish).
nodes.co2e_note: ~4.2 kg CO₂e/shirt for spinning, knitting, dyeing, and sew. Dyeing/finishing often dominates.

nodes.label: Recycle
nodes.summary: Collect, sort, shred / pulp → rejoin Transformation
nodes.details:
Post-consumer collection → sort by fibre → mechanical shredding (or limited chemical pulp) → re-spin / re-knit into fabric back into Transformation.

Figures are remanufacture emissions — not a credit for avoiding virgin cotton. Mechanical recycle usually downcycles; blends and elastane limit quality.
nodes.co2e_note: ~1.6 kg CO₂e/shirt for collect/sort plus mechanical fibre recovery and re-knitting. Lower than virgin mill path, still a positive emission.

nodes.label: Reuse
nodes.summary: Donate, thrift, repair — then back into Transformation / wear
nodes.details:
Second-hand: reverse logistics → sort/grade → repair if needed → rejoin retail Transformation or direct re-wear.

Each reuse trip still emits (haul + sorting), but far less than fibre recovery or new farming + milling.
nodes.co2e_note: ~0.25 kg CO₂e/shirt per reuse trip (collection + thrift logistics + light repair). Still emits, but less than recycle.

nodes.label: Transformation
nodes.summary: Turn ready material into its final form for the end user
nodes.details:
Material arriving from Manufacture, Recycle, or Reuse is transformed into the finished garment the wearer receives (label, pack, retail finish).

Inbound blanks and outbound retail freight are Deliver edges, not this node.
nodes.co2e_note: ~0.2 kg CO₂e/shirt for final finish, label, and pack attributed to the garment.

nodes.label: Use
nodes.summary: Wear; then choose landfill, recycle, or reuse
nodes.details:
Washing dominates use-phase GHG for cotton, but pathway choice at end of life still decides whether the next shirt needs virgin fibre.

• Landfill — material lost
• Recycle — fibre recovery / rejoin Transformation
• Reuse — thrift/repair and rejoin Transformation
nodes.co2e_note: ~0.8 kg CO₂e/shirt illustrative laundry over a short wear life (wash/dry mix). Pathway choice still dominates long-run material demand.

co2e_lines.label: Laundry over wear life
co2e_lines.note: Varies with wash temp and dryer use

nodes.label: Landfill
nodes.details: Residual-waste collection and burial/incineration. Cotton can generate methane in landfill; embodied carbon and fibre are lost permanently.
nodes.co2e_note: ~0.15 kg CO₂e/shirt collection + landfill/incineration ops. Climate cost of this route is also that the next shirt needs virgin manufacture.

edges.co2e_note: ~0.15 kg CO₂e/shirt regional blank / garment haul to finish/pack.
~0.25 kg CO₂e/shirt outbound retail / e-commerce chain.
